one_line_takeaway: LLM-Virus turns an LLM into an evolutionary operator to breed transferable, low-perplexity, low-cost black-box jailbreak templates that beat prior attacks on open-source and weaker closed-source models, but still stalls on the most safety-aligned targets (GPT-4, Claude).
key_finding: On HarmBench (ASRc, fine-tuned Llama-2-13b classifier) LLM-Virus is best on all open-source models and 3 of 5 closed-source models, e.g. Vicuna-13B 91.8, Vicuna-7B 80.5, Llama-2-70B-Chat 60.5, GPT-3.5-Turbo-0613 71.8 (vs best baseline TAP-T 62.3), Gemini-Pro 56.8 (1.46x the second-best); averaged over three Llama-3.1 scales it reaches ASRc 44.2 versus 1.37 for the other EA method AutoDAN. On AdvBench it reaches ASRl of 96.5 (GPT-3.5-Turbo, >2x BlackDAN), 96.6 (Llama-2-7B), 97.0 (Vicuna-7B), 36.5 (GPT-4). It is also cheaper and stealthier than gradient methods: perplexity 45.1 (vs GCG 1532.2, close to AutoDAN 46.5 and handcrafted DAN 23.0) and 1.2 min/sample with 5 parallel workers (vs GCG 15 min, AutoDAN 12 min, BlackDAN 2 min).
core_contribution: The paper proposes LLM-Virus, a black-box jailbreak attack that frames jailbreaking as a combined evolutionary-optimization and transfer-learning problem, using an LLM (GPT-4o) as the heuristic evolutionary operator. Its own novel components are: (1) evolving reusable jailbreak TEMPLATES (into which arbitrary malicious queries can later be embedded) rather than per-query prompts, using an analogy where the template is the virus's mutating genetic material, the malicious query is the functional protein, and the target LLM is the host; (2) 'heuristic' LLM-driven crossover/mutation that operate from word- to paragraph-level and are steered by explicit constraint features (stealthiness fs, diversity fd, cheapness/length fc) via tailored system prompts; and (3) a three-stage pipeline (Strain Collection -> Local Evolution -> Generalized Infection) where evolution runs only on a small clustered representative subset Dr of the malicious-query set to cut cost, then the evolved templates are transferred to the full set D. The authors are explicit that they combine two prior research lines (LLMs-as-EA-operators and EA-for-jailbreak); those lines (AutoDAN, BlackDAN, GPTFuzzer, SMJ, LMEA) are cited Related Work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Black-box, inference-time jailbreak. The attacker has only query access to the target/host LLM L (treated as a function T->T) and no access to gradients, weights, or logits (contrasted against white-box methods like GCG). The goal is to find a jailbreak template j such that L(j (+) q_i) returns an affirmative harmful answer t_i for a malicious query q_i that would otherwise be refused. Attacker capabilities include the ability to embed a query into a template and to call a separate capable LLM (GPT-4o) as the offline evolutionary operator/fitness evaluator. This threat model is stated by the paper itself (Section III, Eq. 1).
limitations: 1) On the two hardest closed-source targets LLM-Virus does NOT win: GPT-4-0613 only 29.3 ASRc (vs TAP-T 54.8; PAP-top5 16.8) and Claude-2 essentially fails for all methods (LLM-Virus 1.5). 2) The evolved templates do not transfer to the highly aligned Claude-3.5-Haiku, which is only jailbroken by viruses evolved specifically on it. 3) The attack depends on access to a strong external operator LLM (GPT-4o), so its capability is coupled to that model. 4) The seed population is drawn from an existing human-written jailbreak dataset, bounding the initial search. 5) Possible evaluation circularity on AdvBench, where GPT-4o serves as both the evolutionary/fitness operator and the ASRl judge (HarmBench avoids this by using a separate Llama-2-13b classifier for ASRc). 6) The 2.5% Dr subset and reported best-individual toxicity mean headline numbers reflect the top-performing evolved template, not average population behavior.

datasets_used: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings + KMeans clustering to build representative subset Dr (|Dr|/|D| = 2.5%)
quantitative_results: HarmBench ASRc: Vicuna-13B 91.8, Vicuna-7B 80.5, Llama-2-70B-Chat 60.5, Llama-2-7B-Chat 38.5, Llama-2-13B-Chat 33.5
HarmBench ASRc closed-source: GPT-3.5-Turbo-0613 71.8, GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106 53.8, Gemini-Pro 56.8, GPT-4-0613 29.3, Claude-2 1.5
Gemini-Pro ASRc is 1.46x second-best; Llama-3.1-70B ASRc is 1.61x second-best
Averaged over three Llama-3.1 scales: LLM-Virus ASRc 44.2 vs AutoDAN 1.37
AdvBench ASRl: GPT-3.5-Turbo 96.5, Llama-2-7B 96.6, Vicuna-7B 97.0, GPT-4 36.5
AdvBench ASRk: GPT-3.5-Turbo 90.8, Llama-2-7B 95.6, Vicuna-7B 93.5, GPT-4 74.0
Perplexity: LLM-Virus 45.1, AutoDAN 46.5, handcrafted DAN 23.0, GCG 1532.2
Time per sample: LLM-Virus 1.2 min (5 parallel workers), BlackDAN 2 min, AutoDAN 12 min, GCG 15 min
Local-evolution dynamics: population avg ASRl GPT-4o 11.5->26.9, Claude-3.5 20.8->29.3, GPT-3.5-Turbo 54.6->100.0; template length Llama-3.1-70B >1300->453.8, GPT-3.5-Turbo 461.2->292.2 (36.6% down)
Transfer avg ASRl: GPT-3.5-Turbo 73.7, Llama-3.1-70B 49.3; GPT-4o-mini templates transfer 54.2 (GPT-4o) and 75.0 (Llama-3.1-8B)
Ablation ASRc: full 80.8; no Strain Collection 59.2 (26.8% drop); no mutation 66.9; no crossover 71.5
Case study: last-generation template +57.2% ASRc and -29.0% character length vs first generation
Hyperparameters: N=10, G=10, temperature=1, p_mutation=0.5, p_crossover=1, three-run average
